hypotheses: Hypothesis 1: Keeping the expected return constant, outcome variance will not influence an individual’s contribution toward a public good as long as the distribution of possible outcomes includes gains-only prospects.

Hypothesis 2: Keeping the expected return constant, outcome variance will decrease an individual’s contributions toward a public good only when the distribution of possible outcomes includes the prospect of losses.

Hypothesis 3a: Outcome distributions including the prospect of losses-and-gains (compared to outcome distributions including only the prospect of gains) will increase the likelihood of loss aversion as a determinant of contribution behavior.

Hypothesis 3b: Outcome distributions including the prospect of losses-and-gains (compared to outcome distributions including only the prospect of gains) will increase the likelihood of fear of others’ non-contribution as a determinant of contribution behavior.
claim.statement: Contributions to public goods are premised on the expectation that the collective will realize benefit in excess of the value of required contributions.
claim.statement_location: Abstract section, Page 1 of the original paper

data.wave_or_subset: Study 1 and Study 2, with varied experimental conditions
data.notes: Participants were pooled for each session and choices were combined at random with those of other participants from previous sessions.

method.description: Two studies were conducted using step-level and assurance public goods dilemmas to explore the effects of outcome variance on contributions.
steps: Participants were given instructions and participated in a series of public goods games.
Contributions were made privately and decisions combined with others to form groups.
Contribution decisions were rewarded based on outcomes derived from manipulated conditions of outcome variance and social uncertainty.
method.models: Repeated-measures ANOVA and Generalized Estimating Equations used for hypothesis testing.
method.independent_variables: Outcome Variance, Provision Threshold, Group Size
method.control_variables: Various threshold levels and variants of outcome variance (no variance, low, medium, high)

results.summary: Outcome variance negatively affects public good contributions when losses-and-gains prospects are present. Social and outcome uncertainties both influence willingness to contribute, with outcome variance causing more significant defection when social uncertainty is controlled for.

numerical_results.value: Participants in the losses-and-gains conditions contributed significantly less (Study 1: 58.98, Study 2: 0.49 proportion)

metadata.original_paper_title: Is it even worth it? The effect of loss prospects in the outcome distribution of a public goods dilemma
